common_misconfigurations.impact: Stale eligible assignments accumulate, expanding the pool of users who can activate privileged access

equivalent_in.aws-iam: IAM Roles with STS + Permission Boundaries
equivalent_in.gcp-iam: Conditional Role Bindings with time-bound access

vendor_configurations.description: Microsoft Graph API permissions control what data and actions applications can access across the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. The permission model distinguishes between delegated permissions (application acts on behalf of signed-in user, constrained to user's access) and application permissions (application acts as itself with tenant-wide scope). High-privilege application permissions like Directory.ReadWrite.All, Mail.ReadWrite, Sites.ReadWrite.All, and User.ReadWrite.All grant sweeping access across the tenant. The user consent settings control whether end users can grant permissions to applications, with three tiers: users can consent to all permissions (least restrictive), users can consent to verified publisher apps for selected permissions (recommended), and admin consent required for all permissions (most restrictive). Misconfigured Graph permissions are one of the most common enterprise security gaps in Microsoft 365 environments.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Set user consent to 'Allow user consent for apps from verified publishers, for selected permissions' minimum. 2. Require admin consent workflow for all application permissions and high-privilege delegated permissions. 3. Audit existing application Graph permissions quarterly and remove unnecessary grants. 4. Use granular permissions (Mail.Read for specific mailbox) instead of broad permissions (Mail.ReadWrite for all). 5. Implement app governance policies in Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps for Graph API monitoring. 6. Block consent to applications requesting high-risk permissions combinations. 7. Use Conditional Access for workload identities to restrict high-privilege service principals. 8. Create an application permissions catalog documenting approved permissions per app type.

common_misconfigurations.issue: User consent enabled for all permissions from any publisher
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users grant malicious OAuth apps access to mail, files, and directory data via consent phishing

common_misconfigurations.issue: Application permissions granted for tenant-wide Mail.ReadWrite.All or Files.ReadWrite.All
common_misconfigurations.impact: Compromised app can read/modify all mailboxes or all OneDrive/SharePoint files in the tenant

common_misconfigurations.issue: No monitoring of Graph API permissions and consent events
common_misconfigurations.impact: New high-privilege permission grants to applications go undetected until breach occurs

common_misconfigurations.issue: Admin consent granted to unverified publisher applications
common_misconfigurations.impact: Potentially malicious applications receive admin-consented tenant-wide permissions

vendor_configurations.description: Authentication Strengths in Conditional Access allow administrators to specify which combinations of authentication methods are acceptable for a given access scenario. Built-in strength levels include: MFA strength (any MFA method), Passwordless MFA strength (Windows Hello, FIDO2, Authenticator passwordless), and Phishing-resistant MFA strength (FIDO2, CBA, passkeys). Custom authentication strengths can define specific combinations. Authentication strengths are used as grant controls in Conditional Access policies, replacing the generic 'Require MFA' control with nuanced requirements. For example, accessing highly sensitive applications can require phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2 only), while standard applications accept any MFA method. This enables progressive MFA strength enforcement aligned to resource sensitivity.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Require phishing-resistant MFA strength for Global Administrators and privileged roles. 2. Require passwordless MFA strength for accessing sensitive applications and data. 3. Use standard MFA strength as the baseline for all users and all cloud apps. 4. Create custom authentication strengths for specific compliance requirements. 5. Combine authentication strengths with risk-based policies (high risk = phishing-resistant only). 6. Use authentication strength requirements to drive adoption of stronger methods (users forced to register FIDO2 to access critical apps). 7. Regularly review and update authentication strength definitions as new methods become available.

common_misconfigurations.issue: Generic 'Require MFA' used instead of authentication strength for privileged access
common_misconfigurations.impact: Administrators satisfy MFA with SMS or phone call, which are phishable and bypassable

common_misconfigurations.issue: No phishing-resistant MFA requirement for any access scenario
common_misconfigurations.impact: All MFA can be defeated by Adversary-in-the-Middle phishing proxies

common_misconfigurations.issue: Custom authentication strength allows weak method combinations
common_misconfigurations.impact: Custom strength definition inadvertently includes methods that do not meet the intended security level

common_misconfigurations.issue: Authentication strength not aligned to resource classification
common_misconfigurations.impact: Same MFA strength required for low-sensitivity and high-sensitivity applications, creating friction or risk

equivalent_in.gcp-iam: Login security settings + Titan key enforcement
equivalent_in.okta: Authenticator enrollment policies with factor type restrictions

vendor_configurations.description: Protected Actions in Entra ID require additional Conditional Access policy evaluation when users perform specific sensitive administrative operations, regardless of their current session's authentication state. Even if an administrator has already authenticated with MFA and has an active session, performing a protected action (such as modifying Conditional Access policies, deleting tenants, changing authentication methods policy, or modifying cross-tenant access settings) triggers a step-up authentication challenge defined by a separate Conditional Access policy. This provides defense-in-depth for the most impactful administrative operations, requiring re-verification at the point of action rather than relying solely on session-level authentication. Protected Actions complement PIM by adding verification at the operation level in addition to the role activation level.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Protect all Conditional Access policy modification operations with phishing-resistant MFA re-authentication. 2. Protect Entra ID role assignment operations to prevent privilege escalation from compromised admin sessions. 3. Require phishing-resistant authentication strength for protected actions, not just standard MFA. 4. Monitor protected action challenges in sign-in logs for unauthorized attempts. 5. Test protected actions configuration with non-production administrator accounts. 6. Document which operations are protected and communicate to administrators. 7. Combine with PIM: eligible role activation (PIM) + protected action verification (CA) for layered defense.

common_misconfigurations.issue: No protected actions configured for tenant-critical operations
common_misconfigurations.impact: Compromised admin session can modify CA policies, assign roles, or delete the tenant without re-verification

common_misconfigurations.issue: Protected actions use standard MFA instead of phishing-resistant authentication
common_misconfigurations.impact: Step-up challenge can be defeated by the same AitM proxy that captured the initial session

common_misconfigurations.issue: Too many operations protected, causing admin fatigue
common_misconfigurations.impact: Frequent step-up prompts lead administrators to seek workarounds or delay necessary changes

equivalent_in.aws-iam: IAM Policy Conditions requiring MFA for sensitive actions
equivalent_in.okta: Admin MFA enforcement for policy changes

vendor_configurations.description: Conditional Access for Workload Identities extends the zero trust policy engine to non-human identities: service principals and managed identities. While traditional Conditional Access only evaluates user sign-ins, this capability enables policies that control workload identity authentication based on location (IP address ranges) and risk signals from Identity Protection for workload identities. Policies can block service principal authentication from outside trusted network ranges, require specific authentication methods, or respond to detected anomalous service principal behavior. This addresses the growing attack surface of machine identities, which outnumber human identities 10:1 in most organizations and are frequently targeted through credential theft, token replay, and OAuth consent phishing attacks targeting service principals.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Create Conditional Access policies for high-privilege service principals (those with Directory.ReadWrite.All or similar). 2. Restrict service principal authentication to known IP ranges where workloads are deployed. 3. Enable Identity Protection for workload identities to detect anomalous service principal behavior. 4. Start in report-only mode to understand service principal authentication patterns before enforcement. 5. Exclude managed identities from IP-based restrictions (their traffic originates from Azure backbone). 6. Monitor workload identity Conditional Access policy evaluation in sign-in logs.

common_misconfigurations.issue: No Conditional Access policies for workload identities; only user sign-ins controlled
common_misconfigurations.impact: Service principals authenticate from any network without policy evaluation; compromised SP tokens usable globally

common_misconfigurations.issue: IP restrictions not configured for high-privilege service principals
common_misconfigurations.impact: Stolen service principal credentials usable from attacker infrastructure without network restrictions

common_misconfigurations.issue: Managed identities incorrectly blocked by IP-based workload CA policies
common_misconfigurations.impact: Azure services using managed identities fail authentication when their egress IPs are not in the allowed list

equivalent_in.gcp-iam: IAM Conditions + VPC Service Controls for service accounts
equivalent_in.okta: N/A (Okta does not manage workload identity policies)

vendor_configurations.description: Entra ID supports passkeys as a phishing-resistant passwordless authentication method based on FIDO2/WebAuthn standards. Two types exist: device-bound passkeys stored in platform authenticators (Windows Hello, Touch ID, Android biometric) or hardware security keys that never leave the device, and synced passkeys stored in password managers or cloud keychain services (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, 1Password) that synchronize across a user's devices. Device-bound passkeys provide the strongest security (equivalent to FIDO2 hardware keys) because the private key cannot be extracted. Synced passkeys trade some security for convenience by allowing users to authenticate from any synced device. Passkeys are integrated into Entra ID authentication methods and can be required via authentication strength policies in Conditional Access.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Enable passkey support in the authentication methods policy for all users. 2. Use device-bound passkeys for privileged accounts (admins, break-glass accounts). 3. Allow synced passkeys for general users to improve adoption while maintaining phishing resistance. 4. Create authentication strength policies requiring passkeys or FIDO2 for sensitive applications. 5. Educate users on passkey registration and recovery procedures. 6. Define which passkey providers are allowed or blocked via attestation GUID restrictions. 7. Plan passkey deployment alongside existing FIDO2 security key and Windows Hello programs.

common_misconfigurations.issue: Passkeys not enabled; users limited to older MFA methods
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users cannot benefit from phishing-resistant passwordless authentication on platforms that support passkeys

common_misconfigurations.issue: Synced passkeys allowed for privileged accounts without restriction
common_misconfigurations.impact: Admin passkeys synced to personal devices or cloud services outside organizational control

common_misconfigurations.issue: No attestation restrictions on passkey providers
common_misconfigurations.impact: Passkeys created in uncertified or unsecured providers accepted without validation

common_misconfigurations.issue: Passkey registration not integrated with identity proofing
common_misconfigurations.impact: Attacker with stolen session can register passkey on their device, establishing persistent phishing-resistant access

vendor_configurations.description: External Authentication Methods allow integration of third-party MFA providers (Duo, RSA SecurID, Symantec VIP, custom OIDC providers) directly into Entra ID Conditional Access authentication flows. Previously, integrating third-party MFA required complex federation configurations or Azure MFA custom controls (deprecated). EAM enables Conditional Access to treat third-party authentication as a native authenticator, including support for authentication strength policies that can require specific external methods for specific scenarios. The third-party provider implements an OIDC-compliant API that Entra ID calls during authentication. This allows organizations with existing MFA infrastructure investments to use their preferred MFA solution while benefiting from Entra ID Conditional Access policy engine capabilities.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Use EAM to integrate existing enterprise MFA investments (Duo, RSA) with Entra ID Conditional Access. 2. Define authentication strength policies that include external methods alongside native Entra ID methods. 3. Test external authentication method integration in a pilot group before organization-wide deployment. 4. Configure fallback to native Entra ID MFA in case the external provider is unavailable. 5. Monitor external authentication method success rates and latency in sign-in logs. 6. Map external authentication method strengths to NIST AAL levels for compliance alignment.

common_misconfigurations.issue: External MFA provider outage blocks all authentication with no fallback
common_misconfigurations.impact: Complete authentication outage when the third-party MFA provider is unavailable

common_misconfigurations.issue: External authentication method not mapped to authentication strength levels
common_misconfigurations.impact: Cannot require specific external methods for sensitive applications via authentication strength policies

common_misconfigurations.issue: Legacy custom controls still in use instead of migrating to EAM
common_misconfigurations.impact: Deprecated custom controls may be removed, breaking MFA enforcement for affected users

vendor_configurations.description: Microsoft Entra Internet Access is the Secure Web Gateway (SWG) component of Global Secure Access that applies identity-aware security policies to all outbound internet traffic. Unlike traditional proxy-based SWGs that use IP-based policies, Entra Internet Access applies Conditional Access policies to internet destinations, enabling user-identity-aware web filtering, tenant restrictions enforcement, and data loss prevention for internet traffic. Web content filtering categories allow blocking or allowing access to website categories based on user identity, group membership, and device compliance. Traffic logs provide per-user internet activity visibility integrated with Entra ID identity context. This extends the zero trust security perimeter from cloud applications to all internet traffic.
vendor_configurations.best_practices: 1. Deploy Entra Internet Access on all managed devices for comprehensive internet traffic governance. 2. Configure web content filtering policies based on user groups and device compliance. 3. Enable Universal Tenant Restrictions to prevent data exfiltration via personal Microsoft accounts on all internet traffic. 4. Integrate internet access logs with Microsoft Sentinel for security analytics. 5. Create Conditional Access policies that apply to internet traffic destinations. 6. Start with audit mode to understand internet traffic patterns before enforcement. 7. Use compliant network conditions to replace IP-based trusted locations.

common_misconfigurations.issue: Internet traffic not routed through Entra Internet Access
common_misconfigurations.impact: Internet-bound traffic bypasses identity-aware security policies; no user-attributed web filtering

common_misconfigurations.issue: Web content filtering policies too permissive
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users access risky or non-compliant websites without restriction or monitoring

common_misconfigurations.issue: Tenant Restrictions not enforced on internet traffic
common_misconfigurations.impact: Users access personal Microsoft accounts and exfiltrate data via unmanaged cloud storage
